notes.note: The physician treating Tiro at Patrae is Asclapo, named in the later letters of this group (16.9); Cicero respects his reputation but distrusts his prescriptions here.

notes.anchor: Broth should not have been given you when your stomach was weak
notes.note: Cicero glosses Tiro's condition with the Greek medical term kakostomachos ("weak-stomached"); ancient dietetics held that broth (ius) was wrong for such a patient, and Cicero second-guesses the doctor on it.

notes.anchor: To Curius, that most agreeable of men
notes.note: Manius Curius is a Roman banker (negotiator) resident at Patrae and a close friend of both Cicero and Atticus; Cicero asks him to take the convalescent Tiro into his own house and to cover his expenses.
A characteristic Roman jibe at Greek unreliability; Cicero fears Lyso, a Greek of Patrae who had been Tiro's host, may be careless, though Tiro vouches for him.

notes.anchor: in the company of the quaestor Mescinius
notes.note: Lucius Mescinius Rufus was the quaestor of Cicero's province of Cilicia; the quaestorship was the junior financial magistracy, and the two men were travelling home together.
Quintus Paconius Lepta had served as Cicero's praefectus fabrum (chief of engineers) in Cilicia and was part of the homeward party; he is a frequent affectionate presence in the letters.
